question_text: Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their boiling points: CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CHO, CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 OH, H 5 C 2 -O-C 2 H 5 , CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 3
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their boiling points:
CH3CH2CH2CHO, CH3CH2CH2CH2OH, H5C2-O-C2H5, CH3CH2CH2CH3
solution_text: The molecular masses of these compounds are in the range of 72 to 74. Since only butan-1-ol molecules are associated due to extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding, therefore, the boiling point of butan-1-ol would be the highest. Butanal is more polar than ethoxyethane. Therefore, the intermolecular dipole-dipole attraction is stronger in the former. n-Pentane molecules have only weak van der Waals forces. Hence increasing order of boiling points of the given compounds is as follows: CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 < H 5 C 2 -O-C 2 H 5 < CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CHO < CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CH 2 OH

solution_text: The carbon atom of the carbonyl group of benzaldehyde is less electrophilic than the carbon atom of the carbonyl group present in propanal. The polarity of the carbonyl group is reduced in benzaldehyde due to resonance making it less reactive in nucleophilic addition reaction as shown below and hence it is less reactive than propanal.

question_text: An organic compound (A) with molecular formula C 8 H 8 O forms an orange-red precipitate with 2,4-DNP reagent and gives yellow precipitate on heating with iodine in the presence of sodium hydroxide. It neither reduces Tollens’ or Fehlings’ reagent, nor does it decolourise bromine water or Baeyer’s reagent. On drastic oxidation with chromic acid, it gives a carboxylic acid (B) having molecular formula C 7 H 6 O 2 . Identify the compounds (A) and (B) and explain the reactions involved.
question_html: An organic compound (A) with molecular formula C8H8O forms an orange-red precipitate with 2,4-DNP reagent and gives yellow precipitate on heating with iodine in the presence of sodium hydroxide. It neither reduces Tollens’ or Fehlings’ reagent, nor does it decolourise bromine water or Baeyer’s reagent. On drastic oxidation with chromic acid, it gives a carboxylic acid (B) having molecular formula C7H6O2. Identify the compounds (A) and (B) and explain the reactions involved.
solution_text: An organic compound (A) with molecular formula C 8 H 8 O forms an orange-red precipitate with a 2,4-DNP derivative. Therefore, it is an aldehyde or a ketone. Since it does not reduce Tollens’ or Fehling reagent, (A) must be a ketone. (A) responds to the iodoform test. Therefore, it should be a methyl ketone. The molecular formula of (A) indicates a high degree of unsaturation, yet it does not decoluorise bromine water or Baeyer’s reagent. This indicates the presence of unsaturation due to an aromatic ring. Compound (B), being an oxidation product of a ketone should be a carboxylic acid. The molecular formula of (B) indicates that it should be benzoic acid and compound (A) should, therefore, be a monosubstituted aromatic methyl ketone. The molecular formula of (A) indicates that it should be phenyl methyl ketone (acetophenone). Reactions are as follows:

question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions. Ethanol, Propanal, Propanone, Butanone Benzaldehyde, p-Tolualdehyde, p-Nitrobenzaldehyde, Acetophenone. Hint: Consider steric effect and electronic effect.
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions.

  1. Ethanol, Propanal, Propanone, Butanone
  2. Benzaldehyde, p-Tolualdehyde, p-Nitrobenzaldehyde, Acetophenone.

Hint: Consider steric effect and electronic effect.
solution_text: The +I effect of the alkyl group increases in the order: Ethanal < Propanal < Propanone < Butanone The electron density at the carbonyl carbon increases with the increase in the +I effect of the alkyl group. As a result, the chances of attack by a nucleophile decrease. Hence, the increasing order is: Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanol The + I effect is more in ketone than in aldehyde. Hence, acetophenone is the least reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions. Among aldehydes, the +I effect is the highest in p- tolualdehyde because of the presence of the electron-donating -CH 3 group and the lowest in p -nitrobenzaldehyde because of the presence of the electron-withdrawing -NO 2 group. Hence, the increasing order is: Acetophenone < p-Tolualdehyde < Benzaldehyde < p-Nitrobenzaldehyde
solution_html:
1. The +I effect of the alkyl group increases in the order: Ethanal < Propanal < Propanone < Butanone
    The electron density at the carbonyl carbon increases with the increase in the +I effect of the alkyl group. As a result, the chances of attack by a nucleophile decrease. Hence, the increasing order is:
    Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanol


  2. The + I effect is more in ketone than in aldehyde. Hence, acetophenone is the least reactive in nucleophilic addition reactions.
    Among aldehydes, the +I effect is the highest in p-tolualdehyde because of the presence of the electron-donating  -CH3 group and the lowest in p-nitrobenzaldehyde because of the presence of the electron-withdrawing -NO2 group. Hence, the increasing order is:
    Acetophenone < p-Tolualdehyde < Benzaldehyde < p-Nitrobenzaldehyde

question_text: Give the IUPAC names of the following compounds: Ph CH 2 CH 2 COOH (CH 3 ) 2 C = CHCOOH
question_html:
Give the IUPAC names of the following compounds:

  1. Ph CH2CH2COOH
  2. (CH3)2 C = CHCOOH
solution_text: The compound contains carboxylic acid -COOH as the functional group and Ph means a phenyl group. The longest chain contains 3 carbon (prop) atom with a phenyl group at carbon 3 position so the IUPAC name of the compound is 3-phenylpropanoic acid. The longest chain of the compound contains 4 carbon atom(but) with a methyl group as a substituent and a carboxylic acid as a functional group. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group with a double bond on carbon 2 so the IUPAC name of the compound is 3-Methylbut-2-enoic acid. The longest chain of the compound has 5 carbon atom(all are saturated (pent)) which are cyclic with the carboxylic group attached and a methyl group as a substituent. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group(carboxylic acid) so the IUPAC name of the compound is 2- Methylcyclopentanoic acid. The longest chain of the compound is a derivative of benzene with the carboxylic group attached and 3 nitro groups. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group so the IUPAC name of the compound is 2,4,6-Trinitrobenzoic acid.
solution_html:
1. The compound contains carboxylic acid -COOH as the functional group and Ph means a phenyl group. The longest chain contains 3 carbon (prop) atom with a phenyl group at carbon 3 position so the IUPAC name of the compound is 3-phenylpropanoic acid.
  2. The longest chain of the compound contains 4 carbon atom(but) with a methyl group as a substituent and a carboxylic acid as a functional group. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group with a double bond on carbon 2 so the IUPAC name of the compound is 3-Methylbut-2-enoic acid.
  3. The longest chain of the compound has 5 carbon atom(all are saturated (pent)) which are cyclic with the carboxylic group attached and a methyl group as a substituent. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group(carboxylic acid) so the IUPAC name of the compound is 2-Methylcyclopentanoic acid.
  4. The longest chain of the compound is a derivative of benzene with the carboxylic group attached and 3 nitro groups. The no. of the chain starts from the carbon atom of the –COOH group so the IUPAC name of the compound is 2,4,6-Trinitrobenzoic acid.

question_text: Show how each of the following compounds can be converted to benzoic acid. Ethylbenzene Acetophenone Bromobenzene Phenylethene (Styrene)
question_html:
Show how each of the following compounds can be converted to benzoic acid.

  1. Ethylbenzene
  2. Acetophenone
  3. Bromobenzene
  4. Phenylethene (Styrene)
solution_text: In the presence of nascent oxygen i.e. [O] along with KMnO 4 / KOH(it is a very strong oxidizing agent which oxidizes the whole alkyl group to carboxylic salt) followed by hydrolysis leads to the formation of benzoic acid. Carbon dioxide and water are formed as byproducts. The oxidation with alcoholic KMNO 4 followed by hydrolysis leads to the formation of benzoic acid. The reaction is given below: Bromobenzene is first reacted with magnesium in the presence of dry ether (Grignard reagent) to give an intermediate which on reaction with solid carbon dioxide followed by hydrolysis leads to the formation of benzoic acid after acidification with a mineral acid. Here the oxidation of styrene with acidic, basic KMNO 4 or chromic acid is followed by hydrolysis leads to the formation of benzoic acid.

  3. Bromobenzene is first reacted with magnesium in the presence of dry ether (Grignard reagent) to give an intermediate which on reaction with solid carbon dioxide followed by hydrolysis leads to the formation of benzoic acid after acidification with a mineral acid.

  2. CH2FCO2H or CH2ClCO2H
  3. CH2FCH2CH2CO2H or CH3CHFCH2CO2H

On the other hand, the -I effect of F decreases the electron density on the O-H bond. Therefore, proton can be released easily. Hence, {tex}C{H_2}FC{O_2}H{/tex} is a stronger acid than {tex}C{H_3}C{O_2}H{/tex} . or F has stronger -I effect than Cl. Hence, {tex}C{H_2}FC{O_2}H{/tex} is stronger acid than {tex}C{H_2}ClC{O_2}H{/tex} or Inductive effect decreases with an increase in distance. Hence, the +I effect of F in CH 3 CHFCH 2 CO 2 H more than it is in CH 2 FCH 2 CH 2 CO 2 H. Hence, CH 3 CHFCH 2 CO 2 H is stronger acid than CH 2 FCH 2 CH 2 CO 2 H or Due to the -I effect of F, it is easier to release proton in the case of compound (A). However, in the case of compound (B), release of proton is difficult due to the +I effect of {tex} - C{H_3}{/tex} group. Hence, (A) is a stronger acid than (B).

On the other hand, the -I effect of F decreases the electron density on the O-H bond. Therefore, proton can be released easily. Hence, {tex}C{H_2}FC{O_2}H{/tex} is a stronger acid than {tex}C{H_3}C{O_2}H{/tex} .

subject: Chemistry
question_text: What is meant by the following terms? Give an example of the reaction in each case. Cyanohydrin Acetal Semicarbazone Aldol Hemiacetal Oxime Ketal Imine 2,4-DNP-derivative Schiff’s base
question_html:
What is meant by the following terms? Give an example of the reaction in each case.

  1. Cyanohydrin
  2. Acetal
  3. Semicarbazone
  4. Aldol
  5. Hemiacetal
  6. Oxime
  7. Ketal
  8. Imine
  9. 2,4-DNP-derivative
  10. Schiff’s base
solution_text: Cyanohydrin: The -OH group and cyano group are present on the same carbon atom. Addition of (hydrogen cyanide)HCN to the carbonyl. {tex}CH_3CHO+HCN\xrightarrow{pH9-10}CH_3CH(OH)CN{/tex} Acetal: Terminal C atom has two alkoxy groups. Two equivalents of monohydric alcohol add to 1 equivalent of aldehyde in the presence of dry hydrochloric acid gas to yield alkoxyalcohol intermediate called hemiacetal. {tex}CH_3CHO+2CH_3OH\xrightarrow{DryHCl}CH_3CH{(OCH_3)}_2+H_2O{/tex} Semicarbazone: Derivative of aldehyde or ketone obtained on treatment with semicarbazide in weakly acidic medium. {tex}H_3CCHO+H_2NNHCONH_2\xrightarrow[{-H_2O}]{pH3.5}CH_3CH=NNHCONH_2{/tex} Aldol: Beta hydroxy aldehyde or ketone(ketol) obtained by condensation of 2 molecules of aldehydes or ketones containing alpha hydrogen atoms in the presence of a dilute aqueous base(alkali). {tex}2CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{OH^-}CH_3CHOHCH_2CHO{/tex} Hemiacetal: Gem alkoxy alcohols obtained by the addition of one molecule of monohydric alcohol to the aldehyde in presence of dry HCl gas. CH 3 CHO + CH 3 OH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH 3 CH(OH)OCH 3 Oxime: Aldehydes or ketones react with hydroxylamine in weakly acidic medium. CH 3 CHO + H 2 NOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH 3 CH + NOH Ketal: Known as Gem dialkoxy alkanes obtained by the reaction of ketone with ethylene glycol in presence of dry HCl. Two alkoxy groups are present on the same C atom. CH 3 COCH 3 + HOCH 2 CH 2 OH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 2 C(O C H 2 -) 2 Imine : They contain -CH=N- group obtained by the reaction between aldehydes and ketones with ammonia derivatives such as hydrazine, hydroxylamine, phenylhydrazine, semicarbazide etc. CH 3 COCH 3 + PhNH - NH 2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH 3 ) 2 C = NNHPh 2,4-DNP-derivative : Derivatives obtained by the reaction of aldehydes and ketones with 2,4-DNP (2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone) in weakly acidic medium. {tex}CH_3COCH_3+H_2NNH\;-\;C_6H_4{(NO_2)}_2\;\xrightarrow[{-H_2O}]{pH=3.5}{(CH_3)}_2C+NNH-C_6H_4{(NO_2)}_2{/tex} Schiffs base(substitute imine) : Azomethines obtained by the reaction of aldehydes and ketones with primary amines (aliphatic or aromatic). {tex}PhCHO+PHNH_2\xrightarrow[\triangle]{H^+}PhCH=NHPh+H_2O{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Cyanohydrin: The -OH group and cyano group are present on the same carbon atom. Addition of (hydrogen cyanide)HCN to the carbonyl.
    {tex}CH_3CHO+HCN\xrightarrow{pH9-10}CH_3CH(OH)CN{/tex}
  2. Acetal: Terminal C atom has two alkoxy groups. Two equivalents of monohydric alcohol add to 1 equivalent of aldehyde in the presence of dry hydrochloric acid gas to yield alkoxyalcohol intermediate called hemiacetal.
    {tex}CH_3CHO+2CH_3OH\xrightarrow{DryHCl}CH_3CH{(OCH_3)}_2+H_2O{/tex}
  3. Semicarbazone: Derivative of aldehyde or ketone obtained on treatment with semicarbazide in weakly acidic medium.
    {tex}H_3CCHO+H_2NNHCONH_2\xrightarrow[{-H_2O}]{pH3.5}CH_3CH=NNHCONH_2{/tex}
  4. Aldol: Beta hydroxy aldehyde or ketone(ketol) obtained by condensation of 2 molecules of aldehydes or ketones containing alpha hydrogen atoms in the presence of a dilute aqueous base(alkali).
    {tex}2CH_3CHO\xrightarrow{OH^-}CH_3CHOHCH_2CHO{/tex}
  5. Hemiacetal: Gem alkoxy alcohols obtained by the addition of one molecule of monohydric alcohol to the aldehyde in presence of dry HCl gas.
    CH3CHO + CH3OH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3CH(OH)OCH3
  6. Oxime: Aldehydes or ketones react with hydroxylamine in weakly acidic medium.
    CH3CHO + H2NOH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3CH + NOH
  7. Ketal: Known as Gem dialkoxy alkanes obtained by the reaction of ketone with ethylene glycol in presence of dry HCl. Two alkoxy groups are present on the same C atom.
    CH3COCH3 + HOCH2CH2OH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH3)2C(OCH2-)2
  8. Imine : They contain -CH=N- group obtained by the reaction between aldehydes and ketones with ammonia derivatives such as hydrazine, hydroxylamine, phenylhydrazine, semicarbazide etc.
    CH3COCH3 + PhNH - NH2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH3)2C = NNHPh
  9. 2,4-DNP-derivative : Derivatives obtained by the reaction of aldehydes and ketones with 2,4-DNP (2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone) in weakly acidic medium.

question_text: Which of the following compounds would undergo aldol condensation, which the Cannizzaro reaction and which neither? Write the structures of the expected products of aldol condensation and Cannizzaro reaction. Methanal 2-Methylpentanal Benzaldehyde Benzophenone Cyclohexanone 1-Phenylpropanone Phenylacetaldehyde Butan-1-ol 2,2-Dimethylbutanal
question_html:
Which of the following compounds would undergo aldol condensation, which the Cannizzaro reaction and which neither? Write the structures of the expected products of aldol condensation and Cannizzaro reaction.

  1. Methanal
  2. 2-Methylpentanal
  3. Benzaldehyde
  4. Benzophenone
  5. Cyclohexanone
  6. 1-Phenylpropanone
  7. Phenylacetaldehyde
  8. Butan-1-ol
  9. 2,2-Dimethylbutanal
solution_text: Aldehydes and ketones having at least one α-hydrogen undergo aldol condensation. The compounds are as follows: (ii) 2-methylpentanal (v) cyclohexanone (vi) 1-phenylpropanone (vii) phenylacetaldehyde These compounds contain one or more α-hydrogen atoms. Therefore, these undergo aldol condensation . Aldehydes having no α-hydrogen atoms undergo Cannizzaro reactions. The compounds are (i) Methanal (iii)Benzaldehyde (ix) 2, 2-dimethylbutanal These compounds do not have any α-hydrogen . Therefore, these undergo cannizzaro reactions. Compound (iv) Benzophenone is a ketone having no α-hydrogen atom. Compound (viii) Butan-1-ol is alcohol. Hence, these compounds do not undergo either aldol condensation or cannizzaro reactions. Structures of the expected products of aldol condensation and Cannizzaro reaction are as follows- Aldol condensation - (ii) 2-methylpentanal- 2-hydroxy-2,4-dimethyl-2-propylheptanal (v)cyclohexanone= 2-(1- hydroxy-1-cyclohexyl)-cyclohexan-1-one (vi) 1-phenylpropanone= 3-hydroxy-2-methyl-1,3-diphenylpentan-1-one (vii) phenylacetaldehyde = 3-hydroxy-2,4- diphenylbutanal Cannizzaro reaction - (i) Methanal= methanol (iii) Benzaldehyde = benzyl alcohol (ix) 2, 2-dimethylbutanal = 2,2-dimethylbutan-1-ol
solution_html:
Aldehydes and ketones having at least one α-hydrogen undergo aldol condensation. The compounds are as follows:
(ii) 2-methylpentanal
(v)cyclohexanone
(vi) 1-phenylpropanone
(vii) phenylacetaldehyde
These compounds contain one or more α-hydrogen atoms. Therefore, these undergo aldol condensation.
Aldehydes having no α-hydrogen atoms undergo Cannizzaro reactions. The compounds are
(i) Methanal
(iii)Benzaldehyde
(ix) 2, 2-dimethylbutanal
These compounds do not have any α-hydrogen. Therefore, these undergo cannizzaro reactions.
Compound (iv) Benzophenone is a ketone having no α-hydrogen atom.
Compound (viii) Butan-1-ol is alcohol.
Hence, these compounds do not undergo either aldol condensation or cannizzaro reactions.
Structures of the expected products of aldol condensation and Cannizzaro reaction are as follows-
Aldol condensation-
(ii) 2-methylpentanal- 2-hydroxy-2,4-dimethyl-2-propylheptanal

(v)cyclohexanone= 2-(1- hydroxy-1-cyclohexyl)-cyclohexan-1-one

(vi) 1-phenylpropanone= 3-hydroxy-2-methyl-1,3-diphenylpentan-1-one

(vii) phenylacetaldehyde = 3-hydroxy-2,4- diphenylbutanal

Cannizzaro reaction-
(i) Methanal= methanol

(iii) Benzaldehyde = benzyl alcohol

(ix) 2, 2-dimethylbutanal = 2,2-dimethylbutan-1-ol

question_text: How will you convert ethanal into the following compounds? Butane-1, 3-diol But-2-enal But-2-enoic acid
question_html:
How will you convert ethanal into the following compounds?

  1. Butane-1, 3-diol
  2. But-2-enal
  3. But-2-enoic acid
solution_text: On treatment with dilute alkali, ethanal produces 3-hydroxybutanal ( Aldol Condensation) gives butane-1, 3-diol on reduction. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} \xrightarrow{{dil\;NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{3 - {\text{Hydroxybutanal}}}\xrightarrow[{({\text{Reduction}})}]{{NaB{H_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_{{\text{Butane - 1,3 - diol}}}{/tex} On treatment with dilute alkali, ethanal gives 3-hydroxybutanal (Aldol Condensation) which on heating produces but-2-enal. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} \xrightarrow{{dil\;NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{3 - Hydroxybutanal}}} - CHO\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3} - CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - enal}{/tex} When treated with Tollen's reagent, But-2-enal produced in the above reaction produces but-2-enoicacid. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - CH}\limits_{But - 2 - enal} = CH - CHO\xrightarrow[{Tollen's\;reagent}]{{{{\left[ {Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}} \right]}^ + }O{H^ - }}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CHCOOH}\limits_{But - 2 - enoic\;acid}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. On treatment with dilute alkali, ethanal produces 3-hydroxybutanal ( Aldol Condensation) gives butane-1, 3-diol on reduction.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} \xrightarrow{{dil\;NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{3 - {\text{Hydroxybutanal}}}\xrightarrow[{({\text{Reduction}})}]{{NaB{H_4}}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - OH}\limits_{{\text{Butane - 1,3 - diol}}}{/tex}
  2. On treatment with dilute alkali, ethanal gives 3-hydroxybutanal (Aldol Condensation) which on heating produces but-2-enal.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}CHO}\limits_{Ethanal} \xrightarrow{{dil\;NaOH}}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {CH}\limits^| }\limits^{OH} - C{H_2}}\limits_{{\text{3 - Hydroxybutanal}}} - CHO\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{\Delta }\mathop {C{H_3} - CH = CH - CHO}\limits_{But - 2 - enal}{/tex}
  3. When treated with Tollen's reagent, But-2-enal produced in the above reaction produces but-2-enoicacid.
    {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - CH}\limits_{But - 2 - enal} = CH - CHO\xrightarrow[{Tollen's\;reagent}]{{{{\left[ {Ag{{(N{H_3})}_2}} \right]}^ + }O{H^ - }}}\mathop {C{H_3}CH = CHCOOH}\limits_{But - 2 - enoic\;acid}{/tex}

question_text: An organic compound (A) (molecular formula C 8 H 16 O 2 ) was hydrolysed with dilute sulphuric acid to give a carboxylic acid (B) and an alcohol (C). Oxidation of (C) with chromic acid produced (B). (C) on dehydration gives but-1-ene.Write equations for the reactions involved.
question_html: An organic compound (A) (molecular formula C8H16O2) was hydrolysed with dilute sulphuric acid to give a carboxylic acid (B) and an alcohol (C). Oxidation of (C) with chromic acid produced (B). (C) on dehydration gives but-1-ene.Write equations for the reactions involved.
solution_text: An organic compound A with molecular formula C 8 H 16 O 2 gives a carboxylic acid (B) and an alcohol (C) on hydrolysis with dilute sulphuric acid. Thus, compound A must be an ester. Further, alcohol C gives acid B on oxidation with chromic acid. Thus, B and C must contain equal number of carbon atoms. Since compound A contains a total of 8 carbon atoms, each of B and C contain 4 carbon atoms. Again, on dehydration, alcohol C gives but-1-ene. Therefore, C is of straight chain and hence, it is butan-1-ol. On oxidation, Butan-1-ol gives butanoic acid. Hence, acid B is butanoic acid. Hence, the ester with molecular formula C 8 H 16 O 2 is butylbutanoate. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - OC{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_2}C{H_3}}\limits_{Butylbu\tan oate} {/tex} All the given reactions can be explained by the following equations.

question_text: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated: Acetaldehyde, Acetone, Di-tert-butyl ketone, Methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN) CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH, CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH, (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH, CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH (acid strength) Benzoic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxybenzoic acid (acid strength)
question_html:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their property as indicated:

  1. Acetaldehyde, Acetone, Di-tert-butyl ketone, Methyl tert-butyl ketone (reactivity towards HCN)
  2. CH3CH2CH(Br)COOH, CH3CH(Br)CH2COOH, (CH3)2CHCOOH, CH3CH2CH2COOH (acid strength)
  3. Benzoic acid, 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxybenzoic acid (acid strength)
solution_text: When HCN reacts with a compound, the attacking species is a nucleophile, CN - . Therefore, as the negative charge on the compound increases, its reactivity with HCN decreases. In the given compounds, the +I effect increases as shown below. It can be observed that steric hindrance also increases in the same manner (no of alkyl groups). Hence, the given compounds can be arranged according to their increasing reactivities toward HCN as: Di-tert-butyl ketone < Methyl tert-butyl ketone < Acetone < Acetaldehyde After losing a proton, carboxylic acids gain a negative charge as shown: {tex}R - COOH \to R - CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} Now, any group that will help stabilise the negative charge will increase the stability of the carboxylate ion and as a result, will increase the strength of the acid. Thus, groups having +I effect will decrease the strength of the acids and groups having - I effect will increase the strength of the acids. In the given compounds, -CH 3 group has +I effect and Br - group has - I effect. Thus, acids containing Br - are stronger. Now, the +I effect of isopropyl group is more than that of n-propyl group. Hence, (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH is a weaker acid than CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH. Also, the - I effect grows weaker as distance increases. Hence, CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH is a weaker acid than CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as: (CH 3 ) 2 CHCOOH < CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 COOH < CH 3 CH(Br)CH 2 COOH < CH 3 CH 2 CH(Br)COOH. As we have seen in the previous case, electron-donating groups decrease the strengths of acids, while electron-withdrawing groups increase the strengths of acids. As methoxy group is an electron-donating group, 4-methoxybenzoic acid is a weaker acid than benzoic acid. Nitro group is an electron-withdrawing group and will increase the strengths of acids. As 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid contains two nitro groups, it is a slightly stronger acid than 4-nitrobenzoic acid. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as: 4-Methoxybenzoic acid < Benzoic acid < 4-Nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid.
solution_html:
1. When HCN reacts with a compound, the attacking species is a nucleophile, CN-. Therefore, as the negative charge on the compound increases, its reactivity with HCN decreases. In the given compounds, the +I effect increases as shown below. It can be observed that steric hindrance also increases in the same manner (no of alkyl groups).

    Hence, the given compounds can be arranged according to their increasing reactivities toward HCN as: Di-tert-butyl ketone < Methyl tert-butyl ketone < Acetone < Acetaldehyde
  2. After losing a proton, carboxylic acids gain a negative charge as shown: {tex}R - COOH \to R - CO{O^ - } + {H^ + }{/tex} Now, any group that will help stabilise the negative charge will increase the stability of the carboxylate ion and as a result, will increase the strength of the acid. Thus, groups having +I effect will decrease the strength of the acids and groups having - I effect will increase the strength of the acids. In the given compounds, -CH3 group has +I effect and Br - group has - I effect. Thus, acids containing Br - are stronger. Now, the +I effect of isopropyl group is more than that of n-propyl group. Hence, (CH3)2CHCOOH is a weaker acid than CH3CH2CH2COOH. Also, the - I effect grows weaker as distance increases. Hence, CH3CH(Br)CH2COOH is a weaker acid than CH3CH2CH(Br)COOH. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as:
    (CH3)2CHCOOH < CH3CH2CH2COOH <  CH3CH(Br)CH2COOH  < CH3CH2CH(Br)COOH.
  3. As we have seen in the previous case, electron-donating groups decrease the strengths of acids, while electron-withdrawing groups increase the strengths of acids. As methoxy group is an electron-donating group, 4-methoxybenzoic acid is a weaker acid than benzoic acid. Nitro group is an electron-withdrawing group and will increase the strengths of acids. As 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid contains two nitro groups, it is a slightly stronger acid than 4-nitrobenzoic acid. Hence, the strengths of the given acids increase as: 4-Methoxybenzoic acid < Benzoic acid < 4-Nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid.

subject: Chemistry
question_text: Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds. Propanal and Propanone Acetophenone and Benzophenone Phenol and Benzoic acid Benzoic acid and Ethyl benzoate Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone Ethanal and Propanal
question_html:
Give simple chemical tests to distinguish between the following pairs of compounds.

  1. Propanal and Propanone
  2. Acetophenone and Benzophenone
  3. Phenol and Benzoic acid
  4. Benzoic acid and Ethyl benzoate
  5. Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one
  6. Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone
  7. Ethanal and Propanal
solution_text: Propanal and propanone : Propanal gives silver mirror test with Tollen's reagent as it oxidised to propanoic acid. Propanone does not give this test. Propanal gives red ppt with Fehling's solution but propanone being ketone does not give this test. Acetophenone and Benzophenone : Acetophenone being methyl ketone gives yellow ppt of iodoform with an alkaline solution of iodine (iodoform test) and Benzophenone doe not give this test. Phenol and Benzoic acid : Phenol does not react with sodium hydrogen carbonate and benzoic acid gives effervescence of carbon dioxide gas upon reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate. Phenol gives violet colour with ferric chloride solution but benzoic acid does not give such colour. Benzoic acid and Ethyl benzoate : Ethyl benzoate does not react with sodium hydrogen carbonate and benzoic acid gives effervescence of carbon dioxide gas upon reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate. When ethyl benzoate is boiled with excess NaOH, it gives ethanol. Ethanol on heating with iodine gives yellow ppt of iodoform. This test is not given by benzoic acid. Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one : Pentan-2-one is a methyl ketone gives iodoform test gives yellow colour ppt and Pentan-3-one does not give iodoform test Pentan-2-one gives white ppt with sodium bisulphite whereas pentan-3-one does not give ppt. Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone : Benzaldehyde forms silver mirror with ammoniacal silver nitrate solution (Tollen's reagent) and Acetophenone does not give this test. Acetophenone gives the iodoform test. Benzaldehyde does not react. Ethanal and Propanal : Ethanal reacts with NaOH solution containing iodine. On the heating, it gives a yellow precipitate of iodoform while phenol des not react.
solution_html:
1. Propanal and propanone :
    Propanal gives silver mirror test with Tollen's reagent as it oxidised to propanoic acid. Propanone does not give this test.
    Propanal gives red ppt with Fehling's solution but propanone being ketone does not give this test.
  2. Acetophenone and Benzophenone :
    Acetophenone being methyl ketone gives yellow ppt of iodoform with an alkaline solution of iodine (iodoform test) and Benzophenone doe not give this test.
  3. Phenol and Benzoic acid :
    Phenol does not react with sodium hydrogen carbonate and benzoic acid gives effervescence of carbon dioxide gas upon reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate.
    Phenol gives violet colour with ferric chloride solution but benzoic acid does not give such colour.
  4. Benzoic acid and Ethyl benzoate :
    Ethyl benzoate does not react with sodium hydrogen carbonate and benzoic acid gives effervescence of carbon dioxide gas upon reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate.
    When ethyl benzoate is boiled with excess NaOH, it gives ethanol. Ethanol on heating with iodine gives yellow ppt of iodoform. This test is not given by benzoic acid.
  5. Pentan-2-one and Pentan-3-one  :
    Pentan-2-one is a methyl ketone gives iodoform test gives yellow colour ppt and Pentan-3-one  does not give iodoform test
    Pentan-2-one gives white ppt with sodium bisulphite whereas pentan-3-one  does not give ppt.
  6. Benzaldehyde and Acetophenone :
    Benzaldehyde forms silver mirror with ammoniacal silver nitrate solution (Tollen's reagent) and Acetophenone does not give this test.
    Acetophenone gives the iodoform test. Benzaldehyde does not react.
  7. Ethanal and Propanal :
     Ethanal reacts with NaOH solution containing iodine. On the heating, it gives a yellow precipitate of iodoform while phenol des not react.

question_text: How will you bring about the following conversions in not more than two steps? Propanone to Propene Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde Ethanol to 3-Hydroxybutanal Benzene to m-Nitroacetophenone Benzaldehyde to Benzophenone Bromobenzene to 1-Phenylethanol Benzaldehyde to 3-Phenylpropan-1-ol Benazaldehyde to {tex}\alpha{/tex} -Hydroxyphenylacetic acid Benzoic acid to m- Nitrobenzyl alcohol
question_html:
How will you bring about the following conversions in not more than two steps?

  1. Propanone to Propene
  2. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  3. Ethanol to 3-Hydroxybutanal
  4. Benzene to m-Nitroacetophenone
  5. Benzaldehyde to Benzophenone
  6. Bromobenzene to 1-Phenylethanol
  7. Benzaldehyde to 3-Phenylpropan-1-ol
  8. Benazaldehyde to {tex}\alpha{/tex}-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid
  9. Benzoic acid to m- Nitrobenzyl alcohol
solution_text: When propanone reacts with NaBH 4 it will form propan-2-ol. This alcohol is dehydrated in the presence of conc. H 2 SO 4 433-443K to form propene. When benzoic acid is treated with SOCl 2 it chlorinates the acid. After controlled hydrogenation in the presence of H 2 Pd + BaSO 4 , it forms benzaldehyde. When ethanol is treated with Cu at 573 k, it will oxidize to ethanal. When ethanal is treated with Dil.NaOH it will form 3-Hydroxy butanal After Friedel craft acylation of benzene, it will convert into acyl benzene. And further treating with the mixture (conc. HNO 3 +H 2 SO 4 ) it forms m-nitroaceto phenone. When benzaldehyde is oxidized with dichromate and treated with calcium carbonate it forms calcium salt. And after dry distillation (-CaCO 3 ), it gets converted into benzophenone. When bromobenzene reacts with Mg in the dry ether it forms Grignard reagent, further treating with ethanal CH 3 CHO in the acidic condition it forms 1-phenyl ethanol. When benzaldehyde is mixed with the ethanal in presence of Dil. NaOH and then heating it in acidic condition, and after that hydrogenation, it forms 3-phenylpropan-1-ol. Benzaldehyde is mixed with HCN at pH 9-10, and further treating it with Water(H + /H 2 O) at the slightly acidic condition it forms α-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid When benzoic acid reacted with a nitrating mixture it forms m- nitrobenzoic acid further it treated with SOCl 2 it chlorinates the acidic COOH group. And reacting with NaBH 4 /H 3 O further it gets converted into m-nitrobenzyl alcohol.
solution_html:
1. When propanone reacts with NaBH4 it will form propan-2-ol. This alcohol is dehydrated in the presence of conc. H2SO4 433-443K to form propene.
  2. When benzoic acid is treated with SOCl2 it chlorinates the acid. After controlled hydrogenation in the presence of H2Pd + BaSO4, it forms benzaldehyde.
  3. When ethanol is treated with Cu at 573 k, it will oxidize to ethanal. When ethanal is treated with Dil.NaOH it will form 3-Hydroxy butanal

  4. After Friedel craft acylation of benzene, it will convert into acyl benzene. And further treating with the mixture (conc. HNO3+H2SO4) it forms m-nitroaceto phenone.

  5. When benzaldehyde is oxidized with dichromate and treated with calcium carbonate it forms calcium salt. And after dry distillation (-CaCO3), it gets converted into benzophenone.

  6. When bromobenzene reacts with Mg in the dry ether it forms Grignard reagent, further treating with ethanal CH3CHO in the acidic condition it forms 1-phenyl ethanol.

  7. When benzaldehyde is mixed with the ethanal in presence of Dil. NaOH and then heating it in acidic condition, and after that hydrogenation, it forms 3-phenylpropan-1-ol.

  8. Benzaldehyde is mixed with HCN at pH 9-10, and further treating it with Water(H+/H2O) at the slightly acidic condition it forms α-Hydroxyphenylacetic acid

  9. When benzoic acid reacted with a nitrating mixture it forms m- nitrobenzoic acid further it treated with SOCl2 it chlorinates the acidic COOH group. And reacting with NaBH4 /H3O further it gets converted into m-nitrobenzyl alcohol.

question_text: Describe the following: Acetylation Cannizzaro reaction Cross aldol condensation Decarboxylation
question_html:
Describe the following:

  1. Acetylation
  2. Cannizzaro reaction
  3. Cross aldol condensation
  4. Decarboxylation
solution_text: Acetylation: Acetylation simply involves the addition of an acetyl group to a compound. An acetyl group is made up of a carbonyl group, or carbon double bonded to oxygen, with a methyl group (-CH 3 ) on the end. The part of the acetyl group that's attached to the compound is often represented with 'R'. Example: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3}COCl}\limits_{{\text{Acetyl}}\,{\text{chloride}}} + \mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}OH}\limits_{{\text{ethyl}}\,{\text{alcohol}}} \xrightarrow{{Pyridine}}\mathop {C{H_3}COO{C_2}{H_5}}\limits_{{\text{ethyl}}\,{\text{acetate}}} + HCl{/tex} Cannizzaro reaction: Aldehydes which do not have an alpha-hydrogen atom, undergo self oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) reaction on treatment with concentrated alkali. In this reaction, one molecule of the aldehyde is reduced to alcohol while another is oxidized to carboxylic acid salt. Cross aldol condensation: When aldol condensation is carried out between two different aldehydes and / or ketones, it is called cross aldol condensation. If both of them contain alpha-hydrogen atoms, it gives a mixture of four products. This is illustrated below by aldol reaction of a mixture of ethanal and propanal. Decarboxylation: Carboxylic acids lose carbon dioxide to form hydrocarbon when their sodium salts are heated with soda lime (NaOH and CaO in the ratio of 3 : 1). This reaction is known as decarboxylation. {tex}R - COONa\xrightarrow[{Heat}]{{NaOH\,and\,CaO}}R - H + N{a_2}C{O_3}{/tex}

question_text: Give plausible explanation for each of the following: Cyclohexanone forms cyanohydrin in good yield but 2, 2, 6 trimethylcyclohexanone does not. There are two -NH 2 groups in semicarbazide. However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazones. During the preparation of esters from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst, the water or the ester should be removed as soon as it is formed.
question_html:
Give plausible explanation for each of the following:

  1. Cyclohexanone forms cyanohydrin in good yield but 2, 2, 6 trimethylcyclohexanone does not.
  2. There are two -NH2 groups in semicarbazide. However, only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazones.
  3. During the preparation of esters from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst, the water or the ester should be removed as soon as it is formed.
solution_text: Cyclohexanones form cyanohydrins according to the following equation. In this case, the nucleophile CN - can easily attack without any steric hindrance. However, in the case of 2, 2, 6 trimethylcydohexanone, methyl groups at 2,2,6 positions offer steric hindrances and as a result, CN - cannot attack effectively. For this reason, it does not form a cyanohydrin. Semicarbazide undergoes resonance involving only one of the two - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} groups, which is attached directly to the carbonyl-carbon atom. Therefore, the electron density on - NH 2 group involved in the resonance also decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. Since the other - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} group is not involved in resonance; it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl-carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones. Ester along with water is formed reversibly from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in presence of an acid. {tex}\mathop {RCOOH}\limits_{Carboxylic\,acid} + \mathop {R'OH}\limits_{Alcohol} \overset {{H^ + }} \longleftrightarrow \mathop {RCOOR'}\limits_{Ester} + \mathop {{H_2O}}\limits_{water}{/tex} If either water or ester is not removed as soon as it is formed, then it reacts to give back the reactants as the reaction is reversible. Therefore, to shift the equilibrium in the forward direction i.e., to produce more ester, either of the two products should be removed (Le-Chateliers Principle).
solution_html:
1. Cyclohexanones form cyanohydrins according to the following equation.

    In this case, the nucleophile CN- can easily attack without any steric hindrance. However, in the case of 2, 2, 6 trimethylcydohexanone, methyl groups at 2,2,6 positions offer steric hindrances and as a result, CN- cannot attack effectively.

    For this reason, it does not form a cyanohydrin.
  2. Semicarbazide undergoes resonance involving only one of the two - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} groups, which is attached directly to the carbonyl-carbon atom.

    Therefore, the electron density on -  NH2group involved in the resonance also decreases. As a result, it cannot act as a nucleophile. Since the other - {tex}N{H_2}{/tex} group is not involved in resonance; it can act as a nucleophile and can attack carbonyl-carbon atoms of aldehydes and ketones to produce semicarbazones.
  3. Ester along with water is formed reversibly from a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in presence of an acid.
    {tex}\mathop {RCOOH}\limits_{Carboxylic\,acid} + \mathop {R'OH}\limits_{Alcohol} \overset {{H^ + }} \longleftrightarrow \mathop {RCOOR'}\limits_{Ester} + \mathop {{H_2O}}\limits_{water}{/tex}
    If either water or ester is not removed as soon as it is formed, then it reacts to give back the reactants as the reaction is reversible. Therefore, to shift the equilibrium in the forward direction i.e., to produce more ester, either of the two products should be removed (Le-Chateliers Principle).

question_text: An organic compound contains 69.77% carbon, 11.63% hydrogen and rest oxygen. The molecular mass of the compound is 86. It does not reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and give positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation it gives ethanoic and propanoic acid. Write the possible structure of the compound.
question_html: An organic compound contains 69.77% carbon, 11.63% hydrogen and rest oxygen. The molecular mass of the compound is 86. It does not reduce Tollens' reagent but forms an addition compound with sodium hydrogen sulphite and give positive iodoform test. On vigorous oxidation it gives ethanoic and propanoic acid. Write the possible structure of the compound.
solution_text: Percentage of carbon = 69.77 %, Percentage of hydrogen = 11.63 %, Percentage of oxygen = {100 - (69.77 + 11.63)}% = 18.6 % Thus, the ratio of the number of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms in the organic compound is given as: {tex}C:H:O = \frac{{69.77}}{{12}}:\frac{{11.63}}{1}:\frac{{18.6}}{{16}}{/tex} =5.81: 11.63: 1.16 =5:10:1 Therefore, the empirical formula of the compound is C 5 H 10 O. Now, the empirical formula mass of the compound can be given as: {tex}5 \times 12 + 10 \times 1 + 1 \times 16{/tex} = 86 Molecular mass of the compound = 86 Therefore, the molecular formula of the compound is given C 5 H 10 O. Since the given compound does not reduce Tollen's reagent, it is not an aldehyde. Again, the compound forms sodium hydrogen sulphate addition products and gives a positive iodoform test. Since the compound is not an aldehyde, it must be a methyl ketone. The given compound also gives a mixture of ethanoic acid and propanoic acid. Hence, the given compound is Pentan-2-one. {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_2} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{{\text{Pentan - 2 - ol}}} {/tex} The given reactions can be explained by the following equations:

question_text: Although phenoxide ion has more number of resonating structures than carboxylate ion, carboxylic acid is a stronger acid than phenol. Why?
question_html: Although phenoxide ion has more number of resonating structures than carboxylate ion, carboxylic acid is a stronger acid than phenol. Why?
solution_text: Resonance structures of phenoxide ion are: It can be observed from the resonance structures of phenoxide ion that in II, III and IV, less electronegative carbon atoms carry a negative charge. Therefore, these three structures contribute negligibly towards the resonance stability of the phenoxide ion. These structures have more potential energy compared to other structures. Hence, these structures can be eliminated. Only structures I and V carry a negative charge on the more electronegative oxygen atom(have less potential energy). Resonance structures of carboxylate ion are: In the case of carboxylate ion, resonating structures contain a charge carried by a more electronegative oxygen atom(less potential structure). Further, in resonating structures, the negative charge is delocalized over two oxygen atoms. But in resonating structures I and V of the phenoxide ion, the negative charge is localized on the same oxygen atom. Therefore, the resonating structures of carboxylate ion contribute more towards its stability than those of phenoxide ion( more the charge delocalized, less is the potential energy, more it is stable) . As a result, carboxylate ion is more resonance-stabilized than phenoxide ion. Hence, carboxylic acid is a stronger acid than phenol.
